preamble: In many Japanese dialects, including Standard Japanese (traditionally), the voiced velar plosive [g] is realized as a velar nasal [ŋ] when it occurs in intervocalic positions or word-medially.

examples.note: In Trans-New Guinea languages, the Proto-TNG prenasalized stop *ᵑɡ simplifies to a plain velar nasal [ŋ] in syllable-final and certain intervocalic positions. Kalam orthographic /g/ historically represents a prenasalized stop; across TNG subgroups this series simplifies to [ŋ] in syllable codas, a widespread process in the family.

Enga (Engan branch, Enga Province, Papua New Guinea) regularly shows word-final prenasalized stops simplified to nasals. The velar series *ᵑɡ yields [ŋ] in coda position, contributing to the typical Trans-New Guinea pattern of nasal-only final consonant inventories. Enga is among the largest languages of Papua New Guinea with approximately 230,000 speakers.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Tsujimura, N. (2013). An Introduction to Japanese Linguistics.
